topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my simmering resentment. I stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers mocking me. Sarah, the new analyst, had just presented her quarterly figures, and the comparison was stark. Her salary, a figure I hadn't even considered, dwarfed mine by a considerable margin. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward like ice water. I watched her pack up her laptop, the casual way she tucked it into her bag, and a sharp, unpleasant energy sparked behind my eyes. I needed to make sure she understood the weight of that disparity. Tomorrow, I decided, I would start making my own presence felt, subtly, but undeniably. Every interaction would be a calculated move, a tiny adjustment to the landscape, designed to highlight the imbalance she had unknowingly created.

[story 2]

I felt a slow burn starting in my chest, a heat that had nothing to do with the thermostat. When Mark casually mentioned his bonus structure during lunch, the air seemed to thin around me. He spoke of projections and high-tier compensation with an air of easy confidence that felt like a deliberate slight. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I excused myself abruptly, the sound of my chair scraping back against the carpet echoing loudly in the quiet corner of the breakroom. Later that afternoon, I spent hours meticulously reviewing every project I had ever completed, cataloging every success, every late night, as if gathering ammunition. A plan began to form, intricate and precise, focused entirely on disrupting the smooth trajectory of someone who had taken what I felt was rightfully mine.

[story 3]

The silence in my cubicle felt oppressive, heavy with unspoken accusations. I traced the outline of my desk with a fingertip, the wood cool beneath my touch. Hearing the details of Clara’s compensation package felt like watching someone else score a point in a game I hadn't even realized I was playing. A strange, sharp clarity descended, cutting through the usual fog of workday fatigue. My hands began to move faster, more deliberately. I started volunteering for every difficult task, ensuring my contributions were impossible to ignore, while simultaneously ensuring that Clara's visibility remained painfully clear. I wanted her to feel the constant, low-level pressure of being perpetually overshadowed, a constant reminder of the gulf that had opened between us.
